summary: Gruber addresses Bloomberg's report that Photoshop for iPad is launching with key features missing, arguing that Adobe's mistake was failing to set clear expectations rather than shipping an incomplete product. He distinguishes between 'real' Photoshop (built on the same codebase) and 'full' Photoshop (exposing every desktop feature), noting that many people conflated the two. He emphasizes that the shared codebase is a major technical accomplishment that ensures PSD roundtrip compatibility from day one. Gruber reports from his own sources that Adobe is genuinely committed to the iPad version as a top-shelf professional product with aggressive feature iteration plans.
key_points: Adobe's core mistake was not clearly distinguishing 'real' Photoshop (same codebase) from 'full' Photoshop (complete feature set), leading to inflated expectations
The shared codebase with desktop Photoshop means complete PSD roundtrip compatibility on day one and that future feature additions are primarily UI work rather than engine work
Gruber's sources indicate Adobe views Photoshop for iPad as a serious long-term investment with a growing engineering team and aggressive iteration plans
Photoshop for iPad is characterized as a genuine rethinking for touch-first UI, not a port, which matters for its long-term viability
money_quotes: When Adobe described it as 'real' Photoshop, what a lot of people heard was 'full' Photoshop, and that was never the plan.
Photoshop for iPad is real because it is using the same code base that's been running on the desktop for decades. That's an amazing technical accomplishment.
Photoshop for iPad is not full — and the initial release was never planned to be — because it only exposes a subset of features from the desktop version.
I'm not suggesting that UI work is easy or quick (it's neither), but the biggest and most important work getting Photoshop for iPad out the door is at the foundational level.
It's a foundation meant to last for a decade or more.

Anyone who cares about Photoshop for iPad as a long-term product should be very excited about its foundation, direction, and the attention Adobe is paying to the fine details of a touch-first Photoshop UI.
Photoshop for iPad is not a 'port' (like Photoshop for Windows was, back in the day). It's a rethinking of the app for modern UI surfaces.
key_insight: Shipping the real Photoshop codebase on iPad with fewer features is the right long-term strategy because the hard foundational work enables rapid future iteration, even if the initial release disappoints those expecting the full desktop feature set.
